professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been discussing how governments fund public broadcasting this semester. Several countries finance national radio and television through a mandatory annual fee charged to every household that owns a receiving device, regardless of whether they watch those channels. Supporters argue this guarantees independent journalism that isn't beholden to advertisers, while critics insist it's unfair to compel people to pay for programming they may never use. Should public broadcasters be funded through a universal household fee, or should they compete for revenue like any other channel? What do you think?

students.name: Lena
students.text: I think the universal fee is justified. Quality reporting is expensive, and once you make a broadcaster chase advertisers or subscriptions, it inevitably starts shaping coverage around whatever keeps audiences clicking. A stable, shared source of funding protects the kind of unglamorous investigative work that commercial outlets simply abandon.

students.name: Marco
students.text: Charging everyone the same fee feels increasingly outdated to me. Plenty of younger households consume news entirely through streaming and never touch traditional broadcasts, yet they're still billed. A subscription model would let the broadcaster prove its value and force it to stay genuinely relevant rather than relying on a guaranteed cheque.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: For this week's discussion, let's think about how museums handle artifacts that were removed from their countries of origin during colonial periods. A number of major institutions have begun returning sculptures, manuscripts, and ceremonial objects to their nations of origin, while others argue these pieces are better preserved and seen by far more visitors where they currently sit. When the original context is contested, should museums prioritize returning such artifacts to their place of origin, or keeping them accessible to a global audience? Defend your position.

students.name: Aisha
students.text: Repatriation should clearly take priority. These objects carry meaning that's inseparable from the communities that created them, and displaying them abroad strips away that significance and treats heritage as a trophy. Returning them acknowledges a historical wrong and lets descendants reconnect with their own cultural identity.

students.name: Jonah
students.text: I'd be more cautious here. Many origin sites lack the climate control and security needed to safeguard fragile artifacts, and a centralized museum exposes the work to millions who'd otherwise never encounter it. Shared stewardship or long-term loans strike me as a more constructive compromise than wholesale return.

professor.name: Dr. Novak
professor.text: We've been examining how software companies handle the personal data they collect. A growing number of firms now advertise products as 'privacy-first', deliberately storing as little user information as possible and processing data on the device itself rather than on company servers. Some engineers argue this is the only responsible default, while others counter that limiting data collection cripples the personalization and reliability that users actually expect. Should software be designed to minimize data collection by default, even when richer data would noticeably improve the product? Why?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: Minimal collection should absolutely be the default. Every record a company stores is a record that can leak, be subpoenaed, or be quietly repurposed later. Processing things locally means the user retains genuine control, and frankly most features people value don't actually require harvesting their entire behavioral history.

students.name: Carlos
students.text: I see the appeal, but a rigid minimization rule sacrifices real usefulness. Recommendation systems, fraud detection, and crash diagnostics all depend on aggregated data, and stripping that out leaves users with a clumsier, less safe product. Transparent consent seems wiser than blanket restriction on what can be collected.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: We've been discussing how communities respond to shrinking populations this semester. Some rural towns facing steady decline have begun offering newcomers free or heavily subsidized houses, hoping to attract young families and revive local schools and businesses. Supporters see this as a creative lifeline, while skeptics argue that without jobs and services nearby, such incentives merely lure people into places that can't sustain them. Do you think offering free housing is an effective way to revitalize a declining town? Explain your reasoning.

students.name: Phoebe
students.text: I'm doubtful it works on its own. A house is only worthwhile if there's employment, healthcare, and decent transport nearby, and most declining towns lack exactly those things. Without addressing the underlying economy first, free housing just relocates people temporarily before they leave again disillusioned.

students.name: Andy
students.text: I'm more optimistic about it. Lowering the barrier to settling down can reach a tipping point where enough residents arrive to justify reopening a shop or a clinic. Population itself generates demand, so the incentive becomes a catalyst rather than a permanent subsidy, gradually restoring the town's viability.
